figures.caption: Fig. 4: Overview of HP+EASN-deep hierarchical model and architecture. Left-side illustrates the 2-level nested variable model, where latent variables z1 and z2 are obtained during encoding hϕ and quantized Q. Right-side shows the architecture, a symmetric 2-layer autoencoder composed of convolutional and up/downsampling blocks with non-linear activations.

Fig. 9: Rate-distortion curves for state-of-the-art lossy coders. Learning-based curves for each distortion loss metric: PSNR (left) or MS-SSIM (right) with red regions zoomed (middle). Non-learning-based coders are depicted with dashed lines.
Fig. 11: Bit rate as a function of the number of image patches that have been sequentially compressed.

Fig. 10: Cumulative average bit rate of the distinct coding steps when applying the recursive bits-back algorithm (left) or the optimized version (middle). Average net bit rate of the distinct coding steps (right). The minimum negative bit rate denotes the required initial bits for a single image patch.

Fig. 12: Learning curves of each individual distribution when training on the validation dataset. Notice the inference curves are minimized when our desire is to maximize them. Top: Inference distributions (decoding steps). Bottom: Likelihood and prior distributions (encoding steps).
Fig. 14: Compression and decompression times in terms of the number of latent variables L and the patch size PS over the Bit-swap model.
Fig. 13: Ablation study of the number of latent variables L and the patch size PS over the Bit-swap model.
Fig. 7: Additional bits per pixel of information required to decompress each rate-distortion model of HP+EASN-deep.

Fig. 8: Quality assessment of two blade pictures with high compression rates. The first row showcases the complete original images, whereas the second row presents a zoomed-in image accompanied by two different compression rates. Sub-captions indicate the distortion loss term and the compression performance. Red regions indicated with numbers pinpoint the following artifacts: 1) blocking artifacts, 2) smoothed contours, 3) smoothed textures and 4) visual color scale change.

Table II: Quantitative comparison on blade segmentation with respect to state-of-the-art methods. Models marked with an asterisk only propose segmentation regions without a class; results assume a perfect region classifier.

Fig. 2: Validation accuracy after applying the random forest step with distinct sets of input pixels. The performance is analyzed in terms of the number of local pixel neighbors taken as input n and the distance d between these input pixels. The purple horizontal line represents the accuracy obtained before applying the random forest step.
Fig. 3: Windfarm test performance of each segmentation step. For readability, the plot does not include every windfarm.

Fig. 5: Failure cases on test images after running each segmentation step. First column: input color image x. From second to fifth column: BU-Netv2 ŝBU , first hole filling ŝH1, random forest ensemble ŝRF and second hole filling ŝ ≡ ŝH2 estimations, respectively. On both sides, the same information is displayed.

Fig. 15: Rate-distortion curves per region of ROI-eML; HP+EASN-deep trained for squared error. The error bars indicate the std distortion on the compression performance. PSNR (left) and MS-SSIM (right) quality measures.

Fig. 6: Computational time in seconds of an individual image for each segmentation step. Computational time for the BU-Net, hole filling and random forest steps are reported. The blue line represents the cumulative time.
